controlnote.p: This collection contains 324 published digital objects.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday May 26, 2026 at 18:00

did.abstract: This collection contains the papers of scholar and spiritual and religious practitioner Lex Hixon (Nur al-Jerrahi) and dates from the 1940s to the 2010s. The papers document Hixon's study of religion, as well as his work as a writer, radio host and religious leader. The bulk of these papers relate to Hixon's writing and study of Hinduism, Zen and Tibetan Buddhism, Sufism and Eastern Orthodox Christianity.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 51 and 52 (audiovisual material): Restricted fragile. Reference copies may be requested. Contact divinity.library@yale.edu for further information.
Box 53 (hard drive): Restricted fragile materials. Reference copies may be requested. Contact divinity.library@yale.edu for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is arranged into four series: I. In the Spirit (1970-2010), II. Writings (1959-2018), III. Personal Papers (1949-2010), IV. Religious Organizations (1970-2013).

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Alexander (Lex) Paul Hixon Jr (Nur al-Jerrahi) was a scholar, writer and radio host, who practiced Sufism, Hinduism, Zen and Tibetan Buddhism, and Eastern Orthodox Christianity. He was born on December 25th, 1941 in Pasadena, California. In 1954, he enrolled at the Hotchkiss School, a private boarding and day school in Lakeville, Connecticut. After graduating from Hotchkiss, Hixon went on to study at Yale University, focusing on literature and philosophy, particularly the works of Søren Kierkegaard. In 1966, he began studying at the New School for Social Research. In 1967, he entered the Religion Department at Columbia University in a joint program with the Union Theological Seminary. He wrote his master's thesis on Ramana Maharashi, a 20th century Indian sage, and his PhD dissertation on the Advaita Vedanta text Gaudapada Karika. He graduated with his PhD in 1976.

In 1971, Hixon began broadcasting a weekly radio show entitled In the Spirit on the alternative radio station WBAI in New York City. He interviewed spiritual practitioners from various religious traditions, as well as thought leaders in the burgeoning New Age tradition. In 1980, WBAI changed the name to Body/Mind/Spirit which aired until 1984.

Hixon was a member of several religious and spiritual traditions. Inspired by the teachings of Ramakrishna, Hixon believed all religions to be true. During his studies at Yale, Hixon traveled to South Dakota to visit his roommate Sam Deloria's family. Hixon met Sam's father Vine Deloria Sr, a Sioux Native American and Episcopal priest, who introduced Hixon to both Dakota and Episcopal religious practices. Beginning in 1966, Hixon spent six years studying Vedanta philosophy and meditation practices under Swami Nikhilananda, a senior monk and scholar of the Ramakrishna order of India. In 1976, Hixon began practicing Tibetan Buddhism under Lama Tomo Geshe Rinpoche. In 1979, Hixon met Sheikh Muzaffer Ozak and began studying Sufism, a branch of Islam. Hixon accompanied Sheikh Ozak on Hajj in 1980 and became the head of the Nur Ashki-Jerrahi order. Sheikh Ozak gave Hixon the name Nur al-Jerrahi. In 1983, Hixon began studying with Father Alexander Schmemann at St. Vladimir's Seminary and was accepted into the Orthodox church. Hixon was also immersed in the practices of Zen Buddhism, and studied with Tetsungen Bernie (Bernard) Glassman Roshi.

Hixon's experiences with and studies of each tradition laid the foundation for each of his books. Hixon published eight books throughout his life: Coming Home: The Experience of Enlightenment in Sacred Traditions (1978), The Heart of the Qur'an (1988), Great Swan: Meetings with Ramakrishna (1992), Atom from the Sun of Knowledge (1993), Mother of the Buddhas: Meditation on the Prajnaparamita Sutra (1993), Mother of the Universe: Visions of the Goddess and Tantric Hymns of Enlightenment (1994), Living Buddha Zen (1995) , 101 Diamonds: From the Oral Tradition of the Glorious Messenger Muhammad (2001). Hixon was a frequent lecturer, guest speaker and taught at the New School for Social Research, the Naropa Summer Institute and the Omega Summer Institute. In addition to his spiritual and religious work, Hixon studied flamenco guitar with Carlos Montoya and with sarod player Vasant Rai.

Hixon was involved in various religious organizations. In 1980, Hixon began serving as the head of the Nur Ashki-Jerrahi Order's lodges in Mexico City and New York City, filling in for Sheikh Ozak before taking over fully in 1985 on the occasion of the Sheikh's death. In the 1984, Hixon and a group of other American students of Vedanta founded the Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekananda (SRV) Order, an organization dedicated to public service and the spread of Ramakrishna's teachings. Hixon served on the board until his death in 1995. Hixon was also involved in founding the Zen Community of New York with Tetsungen Bernie Glassman Roshi, and served on the board of directors.
Hixon was diagnosed with cancer in 1994 and died in 1995 on All Souls Day and Jagaddhatri Puja (a Hindu festival celebrating the Divine Mother, goddess Durga), at his home in New York.
Information in this historical note was primarily drawn from autobiographies and memorials of Hixon, as well as other documentation found in the collection. Additional information was taken from:

Hixon, Lex, Paul Gorman, Sheila Hixon, and Bernard Glassman. Conversations in the Spirit: Lex Hixon's WBAI "In the Spirit" Interviews: A Chronicle of the Seventies Spiritual Revolution . Monkfish Book Publishing, 2016.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: This collection was arranged by Hixon's family prior to transfer to Yale Divinity Library Special Collections. Many of the original folder titles created by the family have been retained. Materials were rehoused into archival legal sized folders and archival boxes. Additional contextual information provided by the family was retained, and folder titles were copied and added to the corresponding folders. In some cases, folders were split into multiple folders to simplify contents. In cases where the title was provided with no additional contextual information was provided, the folder was not photocopied.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Lex Hixon (Nur Al-Jerrahi) papers pertain to Hixon's work as an author, radio host, spiritual seeker, religious practitioner and leader. The materials date from the 1940s to the 2010s, with a majority of the materials dating from 1960s-1990s. The materials include manuscripts, printed materials, correspondence, audiovisual materials and photographs. The collection documents Hixon's studies at the Hotchkiss School, Yale, and Columbia and includes schoolwork and essays. This collection contains materials relating to Hixon's studies in Hinduism, Advaita, Zen and Tibetan Buddhism, the Orthodox Eastern Church and Sufism, as well as materials pertaining to religious pilgrimages and rites. In addition to manuscripts and drafts of published work, the collection contains unpublished manuscripts as well as poetry written by Hixon throughout his life. Correspondence, which is present throughout the collection, pertains to the publishing and marketing of his books, correspondence with others on the spiritual path, as well as with spiritual teachers, including Sheikh Muzaffer Ozak, Swami Nikhilananda and Vine Deloria Sr. Additionally, this collection contains administrative files and audio recordings relating to In the Spirit , Hixon's radio show on Pacifica Radio station WBAI in New York, New York. This collection also documents Hixon's involvement with various religious organizations up until his death in 1995, including the Zen Community of New York, the SRV Association, and the Nur Ashki-Jerrahi Order of Sufi lodges in New York, New York and Mexico City, Mexico, for which he served as Sheikh.

Series I. In the Spirit (1970-2010): This series contains materials relating to Hixon's work with WBAI and his radio program In the Spirit , which was renamed Body/Mind/Spirit in 1980. Materials include correspondence, program lists, interviews of Hixon by others, as well as lectures given by Hixon. This series also contains material relating to the In the Spirit Foundation, the Mind-Body-Spirit Festival, as well as Free Spirit: A Wholistic Directory created by Hixon in 1979. Also contains correspondence relating to the creation of Conversations in the Spirit: Lex Hixon's WBAI 'In the Spirit' Interviews , edited by Shelia Hixon. Additionally, this series contains digitized recordings of In the Spirit and Body/Mind/Spirit .

Series II. Writing (1959-2018): This series contains materials relating to Hixon's writings, including published and unpublished manuscripts, early drafts and edited manuscripts. This series also contains correspondence relating to the publishing and promotion of his books, correspondence with religious leaders and notable recipients of Hixon's books. This series includes mailing lists, book orders, as well as files relating to the subject of each book. This series also contains reviews, interviews, and articles and shorter works written by Hixon, printed materials, either produced by Hixon, or containing works by Hixon, as well as a collection of Hixon's early fiction writings. This series also contains self-published books of poetry, incomplete poems, as well as publishing contracts and agreements.

Series III. Personal Papers (1949-2010): This series is comprised of the personal papers of Hixon, such as correspondence, including correspondence from Vine Deloria Sr, papers from Hixon's high school, undergraduate and graduate schooling, Hixon's memorial service in December, 1995, materials from other projects undertaken by Hixon and books from Hixon's library. Materials include correspondence, notebooks, drafts of Hixon's PhD dissertation and Master's thesis, school essays and notes, audiocassettes of talks given by Hixon, and photographs. This series also contains collages, transcripts from conferences, and materials relating to Hixon's musical studies with Carlos Montoya and Vasant Rai.

Series IV. Religious Organizations (1970-2013): This series documents Hixon's involvement with three organizations: the Zen Community of New York, where he served on the board, the Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekananda (SRV) Center, which he founded in conjunction with other American vedantists, and the Nur Ashki-Jerrahi Sufi order, where he served as Sheikh Nur Al-Jerrahi until his death in 1995. Materials in this series include correspondence, clippings, projects undertaken by Hixon and the organizations, talks given by Hixon, printed materials, newsletters and Sufi materials in Spanish.
Additional information about Muzaffer Ozak and Lex Hixon can be found at the Muzaffer Ozak and Lex Hixon Archive Foundation.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Lex Hixon papers (RG 345), Special Collections, Yale Divinity Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into two sub-series: I. In the Spirit files and II. In the Spirit recordings.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are arranged into five sub-series: I. Books (1966-2018), II.Interviews, articles and shorter works (1966-1994), III. Printed materials (1961-2002), IV. Early writing (1959-1995), V. Publishing contracts and agreements (1973-2013).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains published and unpublished manuscripts, early drafts, reviews, correspondence relating to promotion and publication and subject files relating to each book's topic. This sub-series also contains correspondence to Cassia Berman, Hixon's secretary, as well as edited and unedited manuscripts. This sub-series also contains documentation of Hixon's piligram to Mt. Athos, his expereinces while completing Hajj, as well as correspondence with various spiritual teachers including Swami Nikhilananda, Shiekh Muzaffer Ozak, and Guru M.R. Bawa.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series are arranged by manuscript, beginning with Hixon's first book and ending with his unpublished manuscript "His Eyes are the Wine".

did.unittitle: "Correspondence - Cassia Berman about the 1995 edition of Coming Home"
Correspondence - from literary agent and publisher
Correspondence - important responses to being sent Coming Home

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence from notable artists and authors such as Dr. Frederick Franck, Paul Reps, Bill Moyers, Madeleine L'Engle, Joseph Campbell, Zerka T. Moreno, Philip Whalen, Pravrajika Mokshaprana and Mortimer J. Adler.

"Deacon: That the Lord God will establish his soul where the just reposes…"
Draft of "Lenten Letter" ("His Eyes are the Wine")

"Rejoyce, O Bright Dawn Mystical Day", photocopy of handwritten manuscript
"Rejoyce, O Bright Dawn Mystical Day", typed manuscript
Russian Orthodox Church - articles and clippings
St. Vladimir Seminary papers by Hixon
L'Euchariste Sacrament due Royaume by Alexandre Schmemann
For the Life of the World by Alexander Schmemann
Primitive Christianity in its Contemporary Setting by Rudolf Bultman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains manuscripts of shorter works, as well as articles and interviews with Hixon, autobiographies by Hixon and reviews of books written by Hixon.

did.unittitle: "Communication and Communion: Comparing Ritual Media from Jewish, Christian, Moselm, Hindu and Buddhist Ways"
"Joint Citizenship in Parallel Sacred Worlds", Parliament of the World's Religions in Chicago
"What I am doing is giving alternative language and approaches from which we can pick and choose…"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains copies of Hixon's literary magazine, riverrun as well as other magazines where Hixon's early writings and poetry appeared. This sub-series also contains copies of publications where Hixon's non-fiction writing appeared.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: vol 7 no 3

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: vol 1 no 1, no 2, no 4

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: vol 1 no 1-5

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains poetry, fiction and a screen play written by Hixon in the 1960s. Contains self-published manuscripts and poetry books. This series also contains clippings, writing, books and correspondence Hixon sent to Robert P Hastings, a lawyer and friend of Hixon's parents and one of Hixon's earliest readers. These were returned to Hixon's family upon Hasting's death in 1996.

did.unittitle: "Beginning and End, End and Beginning" manuscript
"Jean, John, Jim novel - no title given manuscript"
Jean, John, Jim novel - no titled give, published copy
"Journey from the Lord of Power" a screenplay by Lyndy Saville, Dominic Saville, and Lex Hixon
The Hixon Book poetry book mock ups
The Hixon Book poetry book mock ups
Poems Read at the Wake of Jerome Sauber
The Collected Works of Alexander Paul Hixon, Jr. Through 1966
Socrates and Jesus by Alexander Paul Hixon Junior
Clippings and publications sent to Hastings by Hixon
Clippings and publications sent to Hastings by Hixon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains publishing contracts and agreements between Hixon and his publishers, as well as royalty statements and correspondence from Hixon's literary agent, John White.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into five subseries: I. Correspondence (1962-2010), II. School papers (1594-1959), III. Memorial service (1995-1997), IV. Other projects (1960-2005, undated), V. Books from Hioxon's library (1949-1978).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains correspondence, including letters sent to Hixon by Vine Deloria Sr. about Deloria Sr.'s relationship to the Episcopal Church and expereinces in South Dakota, as well as correspondence with Hixon's secretary, Cassia Berman. This sub-series also contains correspondence sent to Hixon's widow and family before and after Hixon's death in 1995.

did.unittitle: Berman, Cassia - correspondence to Cassia about books

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Cassia Berman was Hixon's secretary

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Originally titled "Miscellaneous letters"

did.unittitle: Correspondence to Sheila from South Dakota, 90th Niobrara Convocation, Hixon's confirmation in New Haven, Connecticut

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains correspondence from Huston Smith, Sally Montoya, Dominique de Menil, W.S. Merwin, Laura Huxley, Jackson Maclow, June Bingham Birge, Deepak Chopra, and a portrait of Hixon by Alan Ginsberg.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains materials relating to Hixon's high school, undergraduate and graduate schooling. It contains copies of Hixon's dissertation at Columbia University, on the Gaudapada Karika, as well as later reworkings by Hixon and tapes of talks and discussions on the Gaudapada Karika. It also includes his master's thesis on Ramana Maharashi. It includes coursework from Hixon's time as an undergraduate at Yale, including essays, writings and notebooks. This series also includes work from Hixon's high school years at the Hotchkiss School, including essays, exams, notebooks, poetry, clippings and printed materials containing Hixon's writings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Tape of talks and discussions on Gaudapada - when Lex when back to make his own 'free philosophical renderings' of the verses"

"Mahayana Buddhist Influence on the Guada School of Advaya Vedanta: An Analysis of the Gaudapadakarika", Columbia PhD dissertation
The Mountain Path: A Quarterly Published by Sri Ramanasramam
"Unpublished and unedited manuscript - expanded translation of the Gaudapadakarika Books 1-4, 3 essays and an interview with Hixon"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Vol LXXXIV no 76

did.unittitle: Patio Pages Polytechnic Elementary and Junior High School, Pasadena, California

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: vol 25, no 1

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains materials relating to Hixon's memorial service at the Cathedral St. John the Divine, including condolence correspondence, copies of the memorial on audiocassette, photographs, a copy of the memorial booklet given out at the event, as well as photographs, obituaries and other tributes conducted by friends of Hixon. Additional condolence correspondence can be found in the Correspondence sub-series.

did.unittitle: Gathering of Friends in the Spirit with Lex Hixon, Cathedral of St. John Divine, photographs by Peter Cunningham

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains materials relating to projects undertaken by Hixon, or groups with which Hixon worked. This sub-series contains cassette tapes, transcripts, writings, collages, and ephemera.

did.unittitle: The Earth Circle Foundation, "The Mind City" by Saskia Friedrich
Miller, Lindsey "The New Sects" in the New York Post

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains copies of books from Hixon's library. Books contain marginalia as well as annotations.

did.unittitle: Aristotle by John Herman Randall Jr.
Being and Time by Martin Heidegger
Calming the Mind and Discerning the Real: Buddhist Meditation and the Middle View translated by Alex Wayman
The Collected Dialogues of Plato edited by Edith Hamilton and Huntington Cairns
Discourse on Thinking by Martin Heidegger
The Enneads by Plotinus, translated by Stephen Mackenna
Gaudapada: A Study in Early Advaita by T.M.P Mahadevan
The Mandukyopanishad with Gaudapada's Karika and Sankara's Commentary by Sri Ramakrishna Asrama
The Philosophy of Wittgenstein by George Pitcher
Plato: The Man and His Work by A.E. Taylor
Reflections on the Talks with Sri Ramana Maharshi by S.S.Cohen
The Teachings of Bhagavan Sri Ramana Maharshi in His Own Words edited by Arthur Osborne

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into three sub-series: I. Zen Commmunity of New York (1975-2001), II. Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekananda (SRV) Center (1970-1996), and III. Nur Ashki-Jerrahi Order (1980-2013).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Zen Community of New York (ZCNY) was founded in 1980 by Bernard Glassman, Dharma successor of Taizan Maezumi Roshi. Hixon served on the board of directors. Materials in this sub-series contain clippings, correspondence, training materials for ZCNY's program of ecumenical studies, as well as talks given by Hixon at the Zen Community of New York.

did.unittitle: Glassman, Bernie; "A House of One People"
"Passionate Inwardness: Human Existence as Revelation", talks given at Zen Community of New York
Talks on Kierkegaard at Zen Community of New York for study program
Transcripts of talks given at Zen Community of New York
Tibetan Buddhism materials prepared by Hixon for Zen Community of New York ecumenical study program
Zen Community of New York training program for ecumenical studies

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains materials relating to the Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekananda (SRV) Center, which Hixon helped found in 1984. This sub-series contains notes from board meetings, articles of incorporation, correspondence, writing by Hixon, and copies of the SRV Newsletter, as well as materials from the SRV Oregon Association of America.

did.unittitle: "But not in Your Vedantic Sense" manuscript
Project ideas - Vedantic Voices, World Wisdom Center
Sarada Ramakrishna Vivekananda Association of America Newsletter

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: No 1 - No 11

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Vol 2 no 1-6, vol 3 no 1

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains materials relating to Hixon's work as Sheikh Nur al-Jerrahi and involvement with the Nur Ashki-Jerrahi Order's lodges in New York City and Mexico City. This sub-series includes correspondence with dervishes, the Circle Nur al Jerrahi Newsletter, dreams sent by the dervishes for interpretation, and ephemera. This sub-series also contains materials in Spanish, which relate to the Mexico City order, Spanish language writings on Sufism and Hixon's ongoing study of the Spanish language. The Nur Ashki-Jerrahi order continues to operate in New York City, led by Shaykha Fariha al-Jerrahi.

did.unittitle: Correspondence - Hixon's illness and Sufi community
"Ilahis of Shaykh Nur al-Jerrahi" Nur Ashki Jerrahi Sufi Order
"Information for the Jerrahi Dervishes" by Sheik Nur
Pamphlets published by Nur Ashki Jerrahi order

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: no 2 - no 6

did.unittitle: "The Countenance and the Heart of the Sheikh"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: vol 1, no 1-3

did.unittitle: "El salat las oraciones del Islam en el nombre de Ala, el compasivo, el misericordioso"
"Los Cuatro Pasos y Los Siete Niveles"
Para Nur Baba con el cariño de Mariam Aleicia
Spanish language Sufi materials - correspondence, photographs, pamphlets
"Un Enseñaza Apreimiante de la Pluma de mi Sheikh"
